verdicts.notes: Fix present: .github/workflows/ci.yml:110 now runs `pip-audit --strict --require-hashes --progress-spinner off -r requirements.lock`. --strict makes any uncollectable/un-auditable dependency FAIL the gate (closing the silent-skip hole the finding describes) and --require-hashes ties the scan to the pinned lockfile. Test test_supply_chain.py:52 (test_pip_audit_runs_strict_and_require_hashes) asserts the exact string is in ci.yml. Discrimination verified: I reverted to `pip-audit --progress-spinner off` in ci.yml -> test FAILED with 'audit gate must run pip-audit with --strict --require-hashes (#130)'; restored -> PASS. No scope/ADR impact (CI-only). CONFIRMED.

Fix IS present and correct in ci.yml:105-108: `while IFS= read -r id || [ -n "$id" ]; do case "$id" in ''|\#*) continue ;; esac; IGNORES+=(--ignore-vuln "$id"); done` — newline-agnostic (the `|| [ -n "$id" ]` keeps the final newline-less line) and comment/blank tolerant. So fix_addresses_finding holds. BUT the test does NOT guard the actual config: test_supply_chain.py:98 runs against a HARDCODED COPY (_PARSE_LOOP, lines 26-36), not the ci.yml content. I reverted the REAL ci.yml allowlist loop back to the buggy `IGNORES=""; while read -r id; do [ -n "$id" ] && IGNORES="$IGNORES --ignore-vuln $id"; done` form and ALL 7 supply-chain tests stayed GREEN. The behavioral test proves the embedded logic is correct (and even asserts the buggy variant drops the id), but it cannot catch a regression of ci.yml itself — unlike #130/#132/#147 which read ci.yml directly. Non-discriminating against the real config => CONCERN.

Fix present and complete: requirements.lock committed (4692 lines, 218 exact `==` pins, 3886 sha256 hashes, generated by `uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes`); ci.yml audits `-r requirements.lock` with --require-hashes (line 110); ci.yml:89-95 adds a stale-lock guard (`uv pip compile ... | diff` fails CI if pyproject drifts from the lock). Tests test_supply_chain.py:61,68,85 discriminate: removing `-r requirements.lock` from ci.yml FAILED test_audit_scans_the_committed_lockfile_not_the_live_env; deleting requirements.lock FAILED both that test and test_lockfile_is_fully_pinned_and_hashed (FileNotFoundError / assertion). All restored, full suite green. CI-only, no ADR-0002/0003 impact. CONFIRMED.

Fix IS present in ci.yml: IGNORES is now a bash ARRAY (line 103 `IGNORES=()`, line 107 `IGNORES+=(--ignore-vuln "$id")`) expanded quoted as `"${IGNORES[@]}"` (line 110), so allowlist entries cannot word-split or glob-expand, and the `case ... \#*) continue` skips comments. fix_addresses_finding holds. BUT same discrimination gap as #131: test_supply_chain.py:126 (test_allowlist_ids_do_not_glob_expand) runs against the hardcoded _PARSE_LOOP copy, not ci.yml. Reverting the REAL ci.yml to the unquoted-string `$IGNORES` form left all 7 tests GREEN. The behavioral test confirms the embedded array logic prevents glob expansion (GHSA-* not expanded against cwd), but provides no guard tied to the workflow file. Non-discriminating against config regression => CONCERN.

The finding's requested fix (tighten `arize-phoenix>=7.0,<18` to e.g. `>=17,<18`, and de-duplicate the langgraph>=1.2,<2 bound across persistence+orchestration extras) was NOT applied. pyproject.toml is unchanged in the entire pass-2 (git diff 1389242..HEAD -- pyproject.toml is EMPTY); arize-phoenix is still `>=7.0,<18` (pyproject.toml:55) and langgraph is still duplicated (lines 60 and 66). The team instead addressed only the reproducibility half via the lockfile (#132), which pins arize-phoenix==17.6.0 for CI installs. That mitigates resolved-version drift for the audited artifact, but a downstream library consumer installing `assay-engine[observability]` from PyPI (no lock) still resolves against the over-wide >=7.0,<18 range — exactly the surface the finding flagged. No test guards the pyproject range or the langgraph dedup (the lockfile test only asserts the LOCK is exactly pinned, test_supply_chain.py:68, which would pass for ANY range). Fix does not address the finding as written and is non-discriminating => CONCERN.

The finding requested THREE supply-chain monitoring controls; only ONE was implemented. (1) SBOM emission: DONE — ci.yml:111-124 emits a CycloneDX SBOM (`pip-audit ... --format cyclonedx-json -o sbom.cyclonedx.json`) and uploads it as an artifact; test_supply_chain.py:91 (test_sbom_is_emitted) discriminates (removing `cyclonedx-json` from ci.yml FAILED the test; restored -> PASS). (2) `.github/dependabot.yml` for automated transitive-update PRs: ABSENT (file does not exist). (3) Automated license/copyleft allowlist gate: ABSENT (no pip-licenses / license check anywhere in ci.yml; only a comment at line 115 mentions license monitoring). So 2 of the 3 requested controls are missing — the finding is only partially addressed. The implemented SBOM piece is genuinely fixed and tested, no ADR/scope regression, but the fix is incomplete vs the finding => CONCERN.

Fix at src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py:114-124: when corpus_hash is supplied and trust_corpus_hash is False (default), the function recomputes corpus_fingerprint(corpus) and raises ValueError('corpus_hash ... does not match the corpus ...') on mismatch; trust_corpus_hash=True preserves the #119 skip-recompute. This genuinely resolves the silent-decoupling defect (the determinism record can no longer bind a corpus other than the one passed, by default). Guard test tests/test_baseline.py::test_build_baseline_reuses_precomputed_corpus_hash:284-300 asserts (a) a mismatching hash raises ValueError matching 'does not match the corpus' (line 286), (b) trust_corpus_hash=True skips recompute (calls['n']==0), (c) a correct hash is verified with exactly one recompute, (d) omitting the hash recomputes. Mutation: I reverted the fix to the old blind line 'corpus_hash = corpus_hash if corpus_hash is not None else corpus_fingerprint(corpus)' and ran the test -> FAILED at line 286 'Failed: DID NOT RAISE ValueError'. Restored; determinism.py and tests/ are git-clean. No scope/regression issue: dataset-agnostic (hashes whatever corpus is passed), strengthens reproducibility, no ADR-0002/0003 impact.

Fix at src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:336-344: bootstrap_tracing() and trace_ctx.enter_context(run_trace_context(corr_id)) now run INSIDE the try: whose finally (pipeline.py:634-641) calls tracker.end_run(run_id, status='FINISHED' if ok else 'FAILED'). A tracing-setup failure after tracker.start_run now ends the started run as FAILED instead of leaking it in RUNNING state (the #110 class of bug). Guard test tests/test_pipeline.py::test_tracing_setup_failure_still_ends_the_tracker_run:458-487 monkeypatches bootstrap_tracing to raise RuntimeError and asserts t.ended=='FAILED'. Mutation: I moved the two lines back above the try: (pre-fix structure) and ran the test -> FAILED 'AssertionError: assert None == FAILED' (run leaked, end_run never reached). Restored verbatim; the #156 tracing block is correct. No scope/regression: pure control-flow ordering of best-effort observability, no methodology/ADR impact. NOTE: this shared worktree is being mutated by parallel confirmers reverting OTHER findings (#134/#138/#144), which caused 2 unrelated full-suite failures; my files (determinism.py, both test files) stayed git-clean and the #155/#156 verdicts are unaffected.

Fix qualifies the absolute 'nothing leaves the machine' guarantee everywhere the finding cited: README.md:81-85, docs/CHARTER.md:35-38 (principle 5), docs/GOVERNANCE.md:128-134, and ADR-0003 L19-28 which now explicitly separates 'Billing model' (no metered API) from 'Data residency' with the exact wording "'no metered API' is **not** the same as 'on-box'" and states the high-stakes tier is off-box. Code-accurate: src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:410 runs `query(prompt=prompt, options=options)` via claude_agent_sdk, which spawns the claude CLI reaching Anthropic over the network. test_discriminates: tests/test_docs_drift.py:18-26. I restored the stale absolute claim 'Everything runs on-box ...; nothing leaves the machine.' in README.md and the test FAILED at test_docs_drift.py:24 (`assert "nothing leaves the machine" not in text`); restored, passes. No scope issue — change is docs-only and strengthens ADR-0003 honesty.

docs/CHARTER.md:3-6 status line rewritten from 'Active development — engine skeleton ... infrastructure seams are stubs being wired.' to 'Engine implemented and composed — ... in place and hardened (ADR-0010)', matching README and the 407-test code reality. test_discriminates: I reverted the status line to the stale 'stubs being wired' text and test_charter_status_is_not_stale FAILED at test_docs_drift.py:33 (`assert "stubs being wired" not in charter`); restored, passes. Docs-only, no regression.

docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:22 and src/assay_engine/__init__.py baseline docstring both corrected: they now describe shipped primitives (similarity/distance, descriptive stats) + determinism harness and explicitly attribute embeddings/clustering/graph builders to the adapter's BaselineBuilder (ADR-0002), not the engine. Code-accurate: grep of src/assay_engine/baseline/ finds NO kmeans/networkx/sentence_transformers/embed builders. test_discriminates: I reverted both files to the stale 'engine ships embedding/clustering/graph builders' text and test_baseline_toolkit_scope_is_honest FAILED at test_docs_drift.py:45 (`assert 'BaselineBuilder' in text`); restored, passes. Guard is presence-only (checks 'BaselineBuilder' substring) but it does discriminate against the exact stale wording cited. Strengthens ADR-0002 dataset-agnosticism honesty; no regression.

Fix itself is correct: docs/METHODOLOGY.md:131-132 and docs/GOVERNANCE.md:58-60 step 3 both changed from flat 'RFC-3161 timestamped' to 'on-box HMAC by default ... an RFC-3161 TSA is pluggable for third-party non-repudiation', matching the shipped LocalHmacAuthority (preregistration.py:130; no RFC-3161 client in src/). fix_addresses_finding=true. BUT the GUARD is only half-discriminating. The METHODOLOGY half discriminates: reverting METHODOLOGY.md to stale 'RFC-3161 timestamped before confirmation' made test_preregistration_timestamp_wording_matches_shipped_default FAIL at test_docs_drift.py:52 (no 'HMAC' in METHODOLOGY). However, reverting ONLY the GOVERNANCE §2 step-3 body text back to the stale 'RFC-3161 timestamped against a trusted timestamp authority' left the test GREEN — because the file-level substring checks `'HMAC' in text` / `'pluggable' in text` are still satisfied by unrelated honest-scope notes elsewhere in GOVERNANCE.md (the §2 implementation-status note L76-103). So a regression that re-introduces the exact GOVERNANCE over-claim the finding cited would ship with green CI. The GOVERNANCE half of the finding is not genuinely guarded. No scope/regression issue (docs-only).

README.md:23-37 quickstart now leads with a '**Not yet on PyPI.**' blockquote and the working from-source path (`pip install -e .`), with the `pip install assay-engine` lines commented as the post-publication path. test_discriminates: I reverted to the stale quickstart presenting `pip install assay-engine` as the primary working path; test_readme_install_notes_not_on_pypi FAILED at test_docs_drift.py:60 (`assert 'Not yet on PyPI' in readme`); restored, passes. Docs-only, no regression.

docs/decisions/ADR-0006 §1 (L21-22) now enumerates the fifth extra `baseline` and the Consequences install example (L42-43) includes it (or references `[all]`), matching pyproject.toml:68 where `baseline = ["numpy..."]` is defined and included in `all`. test_discriminates: I reverted ADR-0006 §1 to the four-extra enumeration omitting `baseline`; test_adr0006_lists_the_baseline_extra FAILED at test_docs_drift.py:67 (`assert '`baseline`' in adr`); restored, passes. Docs-only, no regression. Note: unrelated half-reverted mutations to checkpoint.py/pipeline.py/adjudication.py (other agents' in-flight #134/#138/#144 verifications) were present in the shared worktree; I restored checkpoint.py and pipeline.py and left adjudication.py untouched (another agent appears to be actively working it). None affect my six docs findings.

Both halves of the finding are genuinely fixed in src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py. (a) Per-conn-str serialization: the single process-global mutex was replaced by per-conn_str bootstrap locks (_CONN_INIT_LOCKS, checkpoint.py:70, helper _conn_init_lock:172-178, used at line 236), and the brief meta-lock _init_lock is now held ONLY for in-memory check-and-set in helpers, never across the advisory wait/DDL. (b) Bounded wait: _acquire_migration_lock (186-198) issues set_config('lock_timeout', _MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS) before pg_advisory_lock, default 30000ms via ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS (line 59), so a stuck peer cannot hang bootstrap forever. Two tests guard this and BOTH discriminate. test_discriminates proof: (1) reverting _acquire_migration_lock to drop set_config -> test_migration_lock_is_bounded FAILS ('no lock_timeout set before advisory lock (#129)', test line 470). (2) Making _conn_init_lock return a single global lock for all conn_strs -> test_concurrent_init_of_distinct_conn_strs_does_not_serialize FAILS after 5.22s ('conn_B blocked behind conn_A's advisory wait', test line 525); restored, passes in ~0.02s. No regression: ADR-0003 loopback enforcement (require_local_uri, line 145) untouched; lock_timeout uses set_config(...,false) (session-scoped, matching the session-scoped advisory lock); 63 passed/2 skipped in tests/test_persistence.py with worktree restored to git-clean.

The fix (the new threaded test test_concurrent_get_checkpointer_runs_setup_once, tests/test_persistence.py:544-562) addresses the described GAP in form — it launches 8 concurrent get_checkpointer() calls on a Barrier — but it is NON-DISCRIMINATING, exactly the defect the finding warned about. I removed the bootstrap serialization (replaced `with _conn_init_lock(conn_str):` at checkpoint.py:236 with contextlib.nullcontext()) and the test still PASSED 5/5 runs (setup ran once every time). The race window is too narrow: FakeSaver.setup (test line 402) does no work, so under the GIL each thread completes the cache-empty->open-pool->setup->cache-write section before the next is scheduled. The fix's own instruction ('optionally a small sleep inside FakeSaver.setup to widen the window... Verify it is discriminating by confirming it fails if the lock is removed') was NOT implemented. I proved the lock IS load-bearing only after widening the window: with the lock removed AND time.sleep(0.05) inside setup, an equivalent test FAILS with 'setup ran 8 times'. So the committed test cannot demonstrate the _conn_init_lock/_init_lock matters — a regression that drops the lock would ship green, which is the precise complaint #143 raised. Recommend adding a small sleep in the fake setup to make it discriminating. (No regression/scope issue; this is purely a weak-test concern.)

Fix genuinely resolves the unbounded pool/thread/connection growth. checkpoint.py now caches one pool per conn_str in _POOLS_BY_CONN (line 74), with a fast-path lookup (_cached_pool, 228-230) returning PostgresSaver over the existing pool, a within-lock recheck (237-239) for the race, and the cache write after successful bootstrap (line 272). Repeated calls for the same conn_str reuse one pool (and its worker thread); distinct conn_strs get their own. test_discriminates proof: I removed the fast-path lookup, the within-lock recheck, and the cache write -> test_pool_is_cached_and_reused_per_conn_str FAILS ('assert 2 == 1', test line 455, two pools opened instead of one reused); restored -> passes. No regression: self-healing is preserved because the cached pool's check=ConnectionPool.check_connection runs liveness on every checkout (so a cached pool survives transient outages); failed-bootstrap path still closes and unregisters the pool (test_pool_closed_and_unregistered_when_bootstrap_fails passes); cache write only happens on success. Worktree restored git-clean; 63 passed/2 skipped.

Fix present at src/assay_engine/methodology/verdict.py:45-51: Verdict.__post_init__ now raises TypeError when label is not a VerdictLabel (and ValueError for empty/non-str hypothesis_id) BEFORE freezing evidence — exactly the recommended fix, converting the opaque downstream KeyError in _score into an attributable construction-time error. Test: tests/test_contract.py::test_verdict_rejects_non_enum_label (asserts pytest.raises(TypeError, match='VerdictLabel') for Verdict('h1', label='supported', ...), ValueError for empty hypothesis_id, and that a correct verdict still constructs). Discrimination: I deleted the two guards (kept only the freeze_mapping line) and the test FAILED with 'DID NOT RAISE TypeError' (verdict.py); restored -> passes. No regression: full test_contract.py (18 tests), test_adjudication.py, test_immutability.py all green; the legitimate VerdictLabel construction path and classmethod helpers (.supported/.contradicted/.indeterminate) are unaffected. Scope clean (no ADR-0002/0003 impact).

Fix present at src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:357-362: after the parse() try/except, `if not isinstance(corpus, Corpus): raise IngestionError('parser returned ... expected Corpus')` plus a paired `if not isinstance(source_fp, str): raise IngestionError(...)` — both guards run before `corpus.units`, keeping the documented 'Raises IngestionError on a bad source' contract honest. Test: tests/test_pipeline.py::test_parser_returning_non_corpus_raises_ingestion_error drives a _NoneParser through the real run_study and asserts pytest.raises(IngestionError, match='expected Corpus'). Discrimination: I removed the isinstance(corpus, Corpus) guard and the test FAILED with the exact opaque error the finding names — 'AttributeError: NoneType object has no attribute units' at pipeline.py (corpus.units); restored -> passes. No regression: end-to-end pipeline suite green; the guard accepts genuine Corpus instances (the reference adapter e2e tests pass). Scope clean.

Fix present in BOTH peer records as recommended. StudyDefinition.__post_init__ (src/assay_engine/contracts/study.py:43-46) coerces modes to frozenset and rejects non-StudyMode members (TypeError). DiscoverConfirmSplit.__post_init__ (src/assay_engine/methodology/firewalls.py:101-104) coerces both discovery_ids/confirm_ids to frozenset before the disjointness check, so the invariant can't be defeated by post-construction .add(). Tests: test_study_definition_coerces_modes_to_frozenset (asserts isinstance(d.modes, frozenset), hash(d) is int — would raise TypeError pre-fix — and TypeError for a string mode) and test_discover_confirm_split_coerces_and_stays_immutable (asserts frozenset coercion and AttributeError on .add). Discrimination: reverting the study.py coercion failed the study test (AssertionError at line 139, modes stayed a set); separately reverting the firewalls.py coercion failed the split test (AssertionError at line 156). Both restored -> pass. No regression: full test_contract.py + methodology suites green; discovery() factory and from_partition still work. Scope clean.

Fix adopts the recommended consistent policy: DROP all @runtime_checkable decorators (the seam Protocols were the only ones decorated, giving false-confidence name-only isinstance). Confirmed grep -rn 'runtime_checkable' src/ returns ZERO hits; the prior decorators on versioning.py:DataVersioner, observability/tracing.py:172, reasoning/seam.py were removed (verified via git show 2a5b13a diff showing -@runtime_checkable / -import runtime_checkable replaced by 'Structural Protocol only — behavior-based (#148)' comments). Test: tests/test_contract.py::test_no_seam_protocol_is_runtime_checkable iterates tracing/tracking/vectorstore/versioning/seam and fails if any class still has _is_runtime_protocol. Discrimination: I re-added '@runtime_checkable' to versioning.DataVersioner and the test FAILED with 'assay_engine.persistence.versioning.DataVersioner is still runtime_checkable'; restored -> passes. No regression: grep confirms no isinstance check anywhere in src/ targets these seam protocols, so removing the decorator breaks no live validation path; full contract suite green. Scope clean.

Fix present at src/assay_engine/contracts/features.py:35-45: FeatureMatrix.__post_init__ now iterates every cell and raises TypeError for any non-(int|float) or bool value, and ValueError for any non-finite (NaN/inf) value — mirroring the finite-numeric discipline the finding cited. Test: tests/test_contract.py::test_feature_matrix_rejects_non_numeric_and_non_finite_rows asserts TypeError for a str cell, TypeError for a bool cell, ValueError for NaN, and that a valid numeric matrix (1.0) still constructs. Discrimination: I removed the type/finite loop (left only count/width checks) and the test FAILED with 'DID NOT RAISE TypeError' for the str-cell case; restored -> passes. No regression: test_baseline.py + test_persistence.py + adjudication suites all green (115 passed/2 skipped), confirming legitimate numeric FeatureMatrix construction in the baseline path is unaffected. Scope clean (no dataset-agnosticism break — numeric contract is engine-side, not domain-specific).

Fix: run_study now records the ENGINE-computed claim fingerprint over the materialized/scored claims (pipeline.py:525 `engine_claim_fp = _engine_claim_fingerprint(claim_records)`, recorded at :537 as `claim_fingerprint`), and keeps the source self-report separately for cross-check (`source_reported_claim_fingerprint`, `claim_fingerprint_matches_source`). `_engine_claim_fingerprint` (pipeline.py:109-128) hashes all five ClaimRecord fields (claim_id, subject, referents, assertion, provenance) via hash_value — complete and dataset-agnostic (ADR-0002 OK; no off-box, ADR-0003 OK). DISCRIMINATION: reverted pipeline.py:537 to `source_claim_fp` (the pre-fix self-report); test_recorded_claim_fingerprint_is_engine_computed_over_scored_claims FAILED (recorded value was the lying source's '0'*64 instead of the engine hash). Restored -> passes. Minor design note (not a defect): the suggested fix mentioned raising FirewallViolation on source/engine mismatch; the implementation instead surfaces the mismatch boolean to the human gate rather than hard-failing — the central defect (recorded fp now provably the hash of the scored claims) is resolved either way.

Fix adds within-run id uniqueness via defense-in-depth: pipeline.py:522 `_require_unique_ids([c.claim_id...],'claim_id')` AND adjudication.py:201-208 (seen_claim_ids -> FirewallViolation 'duplicate claim_id'); plus pipeline.py:452 for discovery hypothesis_ids. DISCRIMINATION: reverting ONLY the pipeline guard left test_duplicate_claim_ids_are_rejected GREEN (the adjudication.py guard still catches it). Reverting BOTH guards -> test FAILED with 'DID NOT RAISE FirewallViolation' (duplicate c1 would silently inflate the scorecard). So the test discriminates against the fix as a whole. Restored both -> passes; full suite 442 passed. SCOPE GAP: the finding explicitly named 'the discovery runner' (discovery.py:118-137 discover_and_confirm) as having the same duplicate-hypothesis_id gap; that PUBLIC runner (exported from methodology/__init__.py) is still UNGUARDED — only the pipeline's own discovery loop got the guard. Also there is no regression test for the hypothesis_id vector at all (neither pipeline nor standalone). The central, highest-severity vector (claim_id SourceScorecard inflation through run_study, the assessor's PoC) is fully fixed, tested, and discriminating, so CONFIRMED — but the hypothesis_id/standalone-runner half is incompletely closed.

Fix changes _resample_stability (confirm.py:178-191) to score EACH resample as its own observed statistic against the null and count it stable only if itself significant in the predicted tail (`_empirical_p(null, r, tail) <= alpha`), called at confirm.py:281 with the full null_distribution + alpha instead of `median(null_distribution)`. This measures reproduction of the observed EFFECT, not merely landing on the predicted side of the null center. DISCRIMINATION: reverted the body to the old null-median logic (`reference=median(null); r>reference`); test_whole_corpus_stability_requires_reproducing_the_observed_effect FAILED — barely=[10.0001]*100 (clears null median by epsilon, reproduces none of the observed effect of 100) earned stability=1.0/SUPPORTED instead of INDETERMINATE. Restored -> passes. The genuinely-reproducing case strong=[100.0]*100 still yields SUPPORTED/stability=1.0, and the existing whole-corpus supported test still passes (no regression). Dataset-agnostic statistics; ADR-0002/0003 unaffected.

Fix adds structural duck-typed-mapping detection: _frozen.py:61-79 duck_mapping_items() recognizes objects exposing keys()+__getitem__ that are NOT abc.Mapping; freeze() (_frozen.py:96-98) now converts them to FrozenDict instead of passing them through as live mutable leaves, and the fence scan _contains_interpretation (fence.py:50-52) recurses into them. DISCRIMINATION (both halves, each tested via file-level revert + immediate pytest): (a) removing the _frozen.py freeze() duck branch -> test FAILED at assert isinstance(freeze(DuckMap(...)), FrozenDict); (b) removing the fence.py duck branch -> test FAILED with 'DID NOT RAISE TypeError' (an Interpretation hidden in a duck-map slipped into a Measurement, breaching the one-way fence, METHODOLOGY §6). Restored both -> passes; full suite green. (Note: an in-process monkeypatch of duck_mapping_items appeared non-discriminating due to a stale-module artifact; the authoritative file-level reverts are unambiguously discriminating.)

Fix adds GateReview.payload_dict() (pipeline.py:101-103) returning unfreeze(self.payload), backed by _frozen.py:134-152 unfreeze() (FrozenDict->dict, frozenset->sorted list, tuple->list), giving the public operator-facing GateHandler payload a JSON path while keeping payload itself a deep-frozen FrozenDict (immutable+hashable, #113). DISCRIMINATION: reverted payload_dict() to return the raw self.payload (no thaw); test_gate_review_payload_is_immutable_but_json_serializable FAILED with 'TypeError: Object of type FrozenDict is not JSON serializable' on json.dumps(review.payload_dict()) — exactly the pre-fix regression. Restored -> passes. The test also pins that json.dumps(review.payload) (raw) still raises, documenting the immutability/serialization split. No regression: 442 passed; the public extension point is preserved (immutable record + thaw helper).
